data.title: Compugen: Immuno-Oncology Innovator Poised For A Comeback
data.description:
Compugen (NASDAQ:CGEN) remains an under-the-radar player in the biotech industry despite early and deep investments in computationally driven drug discovery. While big pharma is after familiar checkpoint inhibitors, Compugen targets less-known immune pathways such as PVRIG and IL-18 binding protein. Still skeptical, the market prices the stock as if failure is the default. But with a healthy cash position, a pipeline that reflects evolving immunotherapy trends, and potential upside from partnerships, this mispricing may be over soon.

Financials

Compugen's financials are indicative of a small-cap biotech company that appears relatively flexible. By Q3, 2024, it had $113.2 million in cash and equivalents compared to $51.1 million at the end of 2023. This influx was fueled by milestone payments - $30 million from Gilead (GILD) for the COM503 IND clearance and another $5 million from AstraZeneca (AZN). Such payments introduce variability in quarterly revenue, but highlight a business model that does not rely on dilutive financing to keep operations going.

Despite posting $17.1 million in Q3 revenue compared to $0 the year before, Compugen remains a net loss on an annualized basis. But that loss has narrowed significantly. This quarter, the company posted just $1.3 million net profit versus a $9.9 million net loss last year. This is largely due to lower R&D spending, from $8.3 million to $6.3 million, as some expenses were reclassified under the cost of revenues related to the COM503 program. While reduced R&D costs might signal pipeline stagnation, this is more an accounting shift than a true cutback in innovation.

Without debt and with an apparent burn rate of under $10 million a quarter (which is now on the precipice of turning around as well), Compugen has at least 11 quarters of runway before it would need more capital - extending into 2027 according to management's guidance. This timeline ties in well with key clinical milestones, so the company may be able to stay in the game long enough to raise funds again if the data proves reliable. That removes some of the near-term dilution risk for Compugen, a large concern for small biotech firms. The market undervalues biotech cash positions when they are based on partnerships rather than secondary offerings. Those willing to look past the biotech pessimism usually find that mispricing an opportunity.

Clinical Pipeline

Compugen's contrarian edge is apparent in its pipeline. While the broader immuno-oncology space has been dominated by PD-1 and TIGIT inhibitors, Compugen is targeting the under-explored PVRIG pathway with their lead asset COM701. The industry has seen that single-agent immune checkpoint inhibitors often fail in cold tumors where immune presence is low. Compugen's strategy addresses this head on by targeting a pathway that may be a dominant immune suppressor in some cancers - in breast, ovarian, and endometrial tumors in particular where PVRIG expression is high. The company positions COM701 not as another me-too checkpoint inhibitor, but as part of a larger strategy to turn unresponsive tumors into ones that the immune system can attack.

Compugen is differentiated by its ability to control multiple nodes of the DNAM axis, comprising PVRIG, TIGIT, and CD96 immune pathways. COM701 alone has shown clinical activity, but the company believes its effects can be amplified when combined with COM902, its TIGIT inhibitor. Unlike many TIGIT-focused biotech firms that take a narrow approach to TIGIT inhibition, Compugen's computational approach suggests blocking PVRIG alongside TIGIT might produce more consistent responses. Early-stage data support this idea: the two act in a synergistic way. The ongoing studies comparing COM701 with COM902 and PD-1 blockade will determine if this hypothesis provides a clinical benefit.

Beyond its DNAM axis, Compugen is also developing COM503, an antibody that frees IL-18 from its natural inhibitor IL-18 binding protein. Unlike systemic cytokine therapies with toxicity ceilings, this approach targets local immune activation within tumors, in line with the industry trend toward tumor microenvironment-targeted therapies. The decision by Gilead to license COM503 suggests major players see value in such a mechanism, but the market has not given it much weight. With a Phase 1 trial underway, data will tell whether this bet on IL-18 pays off. Should that happen, a new frontier in immuno-oncology may be open, making Compugen look significantly undervalued in retrospect.

Scientific Background

Immunotherapy is based on an understanding of tumor immune resistance mechanisms. Some solid tumors escape immune surveillance by leveraging immune checkpoints, which act as molecular brakes against excessive immune activation. Although PD-1 and CTLA-4 inhibitors have changed treatment in some patients, response rates are disappointingly low in many tumor types. Realistically, not all immune suppression operates along the same pathways, and some tumors are resistant because they depend on alternative inhibitory circuits. PVRIG was identified as one such mechanism, distinct from the more commonly targeted TIGIT pathway but complementary.

PVRIG mainly suppresses immune activity via its ligand PVRL2, which is upregulated in some tumors that are otherwise poorly inflamed and resistant to conventional checkpoint blockade. As opposed to PD-L1-driven tumors which respond to PD-1 inhibitors, tumors expressing PVRL2 are less inflammatory and difficult to treat with standard immunotherapies. Blocking PVRIG allows Compugen to restore T-cell function in these environments, priming the tumors to become more vulnerable to immune attack. What’s interesting about this approach is that PVRIG is expressed mainly in stem-like memory T cells - an important subset of immune cells for durable responses to therapy. If PVRIG blockade unlocks this cell potential, it may increase response rates and enhance long-term immune memory against tumors, thereby decreasing the risk of recurrence.

The IL-18 binding protein pathway represents a new level of immune resistance that largely remains untreated in oncology. IL-18 is a pro-inflammatory cytokine that should, theoretically, activate immune cells in the tumor microenvironment. Many tumors hijack this system by overproducing IL-18 binding protein, neutralizing the cytokine before it can trigger an immune response. Designed to break this immune suppression, Compugen's COM503 blocks IL-18 from being sequestered, thereby activating T and NK cells in the tumor. This mechanism is particularly relevant given the industry-wide challenge of delivering cytokine-based therapies without systemic toxicity. By localizing IL-18 activation where it is needed most, COM503 might overcome an important limitation of prior cytokine therapies and provide a route to a new class of immune-enhancing agents that need not be broadly systemic.

First Patient Dosed in Compugen’s COM503 Trial

Compugen has dosed the first patient in its Phase 1 trial of COM503, an important step forward for its novel cytokine-based immunotherapy strategy. Unlike the usual checkpoint inhibitor strategies found in immuno-oncology, COM503 seeks to unlock the pro-inflammatory effects of IL-18 by neutralizing its natural inhibitor IL-18 binding protein. It's a very different way to boost immune responses against tumors that may bypass the toxicity limitations of previous cytokine therapies. Although it did not produce a significant market reaction, this trial may prove far more significant than its muted reception suggests.

This trial is designed to evaluate COM503 as a monotherapy and in combination with zimberelimab, Gilead's anti-PD-1 antibody. Choosing zimberelimab as a combination partner is interesting because it allows Compugen to evaluate whether freeing IL-18 in the tumor microenvironment may produce a better immune landscape for checkpoint blockade. Previous systemic cytokine applications have encountered safety issues, but Compugen plans to localize the effect where it is needed most, potentially increasing efficacy with reduced off-target toxicity. The first patient was dosed at the START Midwest research center, an institution known for its work in early-phase oncology trials, suggesting that enrollment could proceed smoothly.

Gilead's involvement also adds more validation to the potential of COM503. Though Compugen runs the trial, Gilead owns the license - meaning the larger company will likely lead later-stage development if the drug proves promising. This structure benefits Compugen by allowing it to focus on early proof-of-concept while preserving its financial runway. The biggest takeaway for investors with this announcement isn't that the trial has begun - but that Compugen is quietly pursuing a novel immunotherapy strategy that the wider biotech sector has largely ignored. COM503 could reshape how the industry approaches cytokine-based treatments if the mechanism holds in clinical testing - giving Compugen an edge over others who have failed.

Potential Risk Assessment

Even though Compugen is scientifically sound, it has its risks. The greatest immediate problem is the novelty of its targets. PVRIG is a logical extension of immune checkpoint research, but it lacks large-scale clinical validation. Like PD-1 and CTLA-4, which have been clearly validated by early studies, PVRIG inhibition has not generated large-scale clinical data demonstrating efficacy. There is a real possibility that blocking PVRIG alone does not induce meaningful responses, or that its role in immune suppression is less central than hypothesized. The company's strategy of combining PVRIG with TIGIT inhibition is supposed to hedge this risk, but the synergy thesis could collapse if either target fails to show clinical benefit.

One more worry is whether IL-18 release from its binding protein leads to an anti-tumor effect. The theoretical basis is strong - IL-18 is a well documented pro-inflammatory cytokine that should increase immune activity. Cytokines have historically been associated with complications, however. If Compugen's strategy of localizing IL-18 activation in the tumor microenvironment mitigates toxicity risks, tumors may possibly find another way to dampen its effects. Immune systems work in redundant ways, and cancer cells have been shown to bypass single-target interventions. Also, IL-18 effects may be context-dependent, with some data suggesting that it promotes regulatory T cell activity under certain conditions that might paradoxically suppress rather than enhance immune responses.

Uncertainty also surrounds the larger industry trend towards combination therapies. Although Compugen is betting on its multipronged approach, no consensus exists on how to layer immune checkpoint inhibition and overlapping toxicities might be a challenge. Previous efforts to combine novel checkpoints with PD-1 inhibition had mixed results, as evidenced by some TIGIT-focused trials. If Compugen's dual-blockade strategy does not improve upon established therapies sufficiently, it could be overlooked in a market where major players such as Merck (MRK) and Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY) dominate with established drugs.

Yet, from an academic standpoint, targeting underexplored immune pathways remains a natural extension of immuno-oncology's next phase. As more tumors resist PD-1 blockade, other immune-modulating strategies are needed.

Valuation and TAM

In addition to what I discussed above, Compugen’s low valuation seems unwarranted given the huge growth expected in immunotherapy. By 2033, estimates show that the global immuno-oncology market will be worth about $284.29 billion, with a compound annual growth rate of 23.12%.

Focusing on particular cancer types such as ovarian cancer helps understand Compugen's potential market impact. It affects about 19,880 women annually in the United States. Since many advanced immunotherapies are available for a cost of $200,000 per year, the annual addressable market for ovarian cancer immunotherapies alone in the U.S. would be approximately $4 billion (19,880 patients multiplied by $200,000 per treatment). This figure does not include other cancers in Compugen's pipeline or potential worldwide market opportunities.

Concerning metrics and ratios, the P/E ratio is well above the sector median, given that the company is largely at a clinical-stage, so it is not particularly relevant. The price/sales and EV/sales ratios are both favorable versus the sector median and should improve if the pipeline pans out. As a whole, Compugen is more of a growth play than a value play, since most of my current projections are science-based.

Future Outlook

Compugen operates in a highly competitive space that the market misunderstands. While large pharmaceutical firms devote resources to well established checkpoint inhibitors, the company is advancing a different paradigm - one that targets undiscovered immune pathways that could reshape tumor responsiveness. It focuses on computational target discovery, which has already provided new insights into immune evasion mechanisms. Controlling multiple nodes of the DNAM axis and manipulating cytokine biology suggests a long-term view that could be very useful if validated clinically. While the data looks good contextualized with the science and mechanisms of the drugs under development, only time will tell if Compugen's vision pans out.

data.title: NVDY: Recent Performance Shows An Unfavorable Risk-Reward Profile For Most Income Investors
data.description:
Investing is often about finding balance. Trying to have investments that successfully risk and reward and help people reach their long-term objectives is the goal of most investors.

Income and dividend investing have become increasingly common with rates having been at historically low rates for most of the last decade, and many ETFs and other new financial vehicles purporting to offer substantive and consistent income have come to market during recent years.

An increasingly common investment is covered call funds. These investments seeking to use a variety of options strategies to offer consistent and significant income, well balancing risks to the principal. One fund that utilizes a unique options strategy of taking long positions with synthetic call spreads then selling primarily out-of-the-money monthly calls options to generate income is the YieldMax NVDA Option Income Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:NVDY).

NVDY has offered investors total returns of nearly 202 percent since the fund's inception in May 2023. The underlying NVIDIA (NVDA) stock this fund strategy is to focus on has offered investors total returns of nearly 386 percent during this same time period.

I last wrote about the YieldMax NVDA Option Income Strategy ETF in July 2024, and I rated the fund a sell primarily because I didn't find the risk-reward profile of the fund as appealing for most income investors. Since I initiated coverage of this fund, this investment has offered total returns of just 1.71 percent, while the S&P 500 (SPY) has offered investors total returns of 8.71 percent. Today, I am downgrading this exchange-traded fund to a strong sell. NVDY's strong initial performance was primarily because of the massive move up in the underlying equity this fund is long. The underlying NVDA stock that this fund is long is likely to be more volatile moving forward for multiple reasons, NVDY is unlikely to be able to sustain the kind of steady and consistent income that most income investors are seeking.

NVDY is an actively managed fund with an expense ratio of 1.01 percent. The ETF has $1.55 billion in assets under management and the trailing dividend yield is 89.63 percent. The fund paid $19.10 per share in the last year. The trailing yield is misleading because this fund's monthly payments are very unpredictable because of how volatile the underlying NVIDIA stock in this fund and the stock's likely unsustainably strong recent performance. There will continue to be significant changes in the implied volatility premiums in the monthly options this fund sells against the synthetic call spreads the ETF uses to take a long position in the chipmaker.

The options strategy that NVDY uses is buy treasury bonds and then borrow on margins against these fixed income instrument to use synthetic call spreads to long NVIDIA that go out 1 to 6 months. The ETF then sells standard options and FLEX options at 0 to 15 percent out-of-the-the money. The covered calls this fund sells expire in one month or less. The payouts are primarily taxed as ordinary income.

The fund's monthly payouts have been very volatile, ranging from nearly $.42 a share in November 2023, to $2.62 a share in March 2024.

The primary problem that most income investors seeking regular income will have with this fund are the significant risks to the principal. NVDY does not protect downside risks, and the NVDA is a more volatile stock, which is why the fund managers chose this company for the covered call strategy these individuals utilize. The net asset value of NVDY fell over 25 percent in just the past year, and is down nearly 8 percent since February 2023. While the total returns of NVDY over the last year are 68 percent, the fund has offered investors total returns of just 1.71 percent since the middle of July in 2024. Options funds such as NVDY want some elevated level of volatility to increase the premiums in the covered calls these investments sell, but too much volatility puts the principal at significant risk.

Most income investors are looking for substantive and consistent income without significant risk to the principal, and this fund cannot offer that balance. The primary reason this ETF has offered investors significant returns since the fund's inception is that NVIDIA has risen by 386 percent since this investment's inception in May 2023. NVIDIA is now facing increased competition from smaller chipmakers using AI technology, and analysts are also expecting the company's growth rate to moderate significantly in the coming years.

NVIDIA also trades at a very high growth multiple of 49.8x expected forward GAAP earnings, the stock will likely be volatile if earnings slow even slightly in the future. NVIDIA's has a risk adjusted standard deviation of 3.5., the company swings an average of 42.99 percent per year.

Most income investors are looking for steady and substantive payouts without significant risk to the principal, and NVDY cannot offer that profile. This fund offered performed very well the first year after the ETF's inception, but those where returns were not sustainable. While this investment will likely have some appeal to more aggressive investors using tax deferred accounts, most investors seeking income should be able to find more appealing options.

Index

Market Review

California munis retreated. Alongside rising Treasury yields and persistent inflation, U.S. bonds broadly declined in the fourth quarter. However, municipal bonds (munis) generally fared better than the taxable fixed-income market, and California municipal bonds outperformed national munis. A third-quarter rally helped most bond sectors, including California munis, maintain year-to-date gains.

Economy remained resilient. The U.S. economy grew 3.1% (annualized) in the third quarter, similar to the second quarter. Other data released in the fourth quarter were generally upbeat. Led by services sector strength, activity in the private sector expanded, while consumer confidence improved, and retail sales increased. However, manufacturing continued to contract, and the job market weakened.

Munis outperformed Treasuries. Muni yields tracked Treasury yields higher during the quarter, but at a slower pace, and most muni-to-Treasury ratios rose. Overall, munis outperformed Treasuries. Additionally, general obligation (GO) bonds fared better than revenue bonds, and shorter-maturity bonds generally outperformed longer-maturity issues.

Supply slowed in the quarter, grew for the year. As expected, fourth-quarter muni supply shrunk compared with the third quarter’s level, as many issuers pushed deals forward to avoid presidential election-triggered volatility. For the year, total muni supply increased 29% compared with 2023, according to Barclays.

Fundamentals remained stable. While municipal credit trends continued to level off, balance sheets for most state and local entities remained fairly robust. Fiscal 2025 budgets for most U.S. states include modest revenue growth and conservative spending plans alongside strong reserve balances and liquidity. Additionally, rating upgrades slightly outpaced downgrades, and defaults remained rare.

High-yield munis outperformed. Muni credit spreads tightened modestly for the quarter. High-yield munis declined but outperformed investment-grade munis for the quarter. For the year, high-yield munis significantly outperformed their investment-grade peers.

Portfolio Performance Review

Security selection aided performance. Security selection boosted performance compared with the benchmark, largely due to our choices in the local GO bond, special tax and tobacco sectors. These choices more than offset negative selection effects in the hospital, private school and private university sectors.

Sector allocations lifted results. Underweights relative to the index in the multifamily housing and tobacco sectors and an overweight in the special tax sector aided performance. These weightings more than offset the negative effects from underweights in the airport, local GO bond and public university sectors. Our high-yield overweight detracted, but security selection in the allocation was strong.

Duration detracted. Our longer-than-index duration position weighed on the portfolio’s performance compared with the benchmark as yields rose. Our positioning along the yield curve, which favored short and intermediate maturities, helped offset some negative duration effects.

Goal and Strategy

Total return and current income that is exempt from federal and California income taxes through high-yield municipal bonds.

Positioning for the Future

Growth may gather momentum. We expect slower growth in early 2025, as Trump administration initiatives, including new tariffs and government efficiency efforts, may weaken consumer demand and employment. By 2025’s second half, deregulation, onshoring and rekindled animal spirits may reignite growth. As the Trump administration's tax policy unfolds, we’ll monitor the potential muni market effects.

Credit backdrop to moderate. We expect previously strong credit trends to continue moderating. However, healthy reserves and sound liquidity should help mitigate the effects of slowing revenue growth and waning federal stimulus funds. We also expect an increase in state and local governments facing larger projected budget gaps, such as those challenging Chicago and Oakland, California.

Focused on quality. Given our expectations for growth to slow, we are maintaining a bias toward higher-quality securities and a slight duration overweight relative to the benchmark. As always, stringent security selection guides our process. With valuations tight and political priorities yet to unfold, we’re focused on select sectors, waiting for better opportunities to meaningfully boost credit exposure.

Commentary Glossary

Adjustable-Rate Mortgages (ARMs): Mortgages in which the interest rate is adjusted periodically according to a specific index.

Agency Securities (Agencies): Debt securities issued by U.S. government agencies.

Agency MBS: Mortgage-backed securities issued by U.S. government agencies.

Alternative Minimum Tax (AMT): A parallel tax system that was created to keep high income individuals from avoiding taxes through various deductions and exemptions.

Asset-Backed Securities (ABS): A form of securitized debt backed by loan assets such as auto loans, student loans, and credit card debt.

Basis Points (BPs): Used to express percentage values that are carried out to two decimal places. One basis point equals 0.01%.

Breakeven Rate: Yield difference between nominal Treasury notes and TIPS; indicates the market’s expectations for inflation.

Carry-Oriented Currencies: Higher-yielding currencies of countries where interest rates are generally higher than those of countries with lower- yielding currencies. These higher-yielding currencies are targeted for “carry trades,” where investors borrow money in a low-interest rate currency and invest in a higher yielding currency, potentially profiting from the difference in interest rates.

Collateralized Loan Obligations (CLOs): A form of securitized debt, typically backed by pools of corporate loans and their payments.

Collateralized Mortgage Obligations (CMOs): A form of structured, securitized debt derived from mortgage-backed securities, typically backed by pools of residential mortgages and their payments.

Commercial Mortgage-Backed Securities (CMBS): Securities representing ownership in pools of commercial real estate loans used to finance the construction and improvement of income-producing properties such as hotels, shopping centers, and office buildings.

Commercial Paper: Short-term debt issued by corporations to raise cash and to cover current expenses in anticipation of future revenues.

Commodity: Basic raw materials such as precious metals and natural resources.

Consumer Price Index (CPI): Published by the U.S. government, CPI is the most commonly used statistic to measure inflation in the U.S. economy. Core CPI excludes food and energy prices, which tend to be volatile.

Contribution to Duration (CTD): A measure of bond portfolio risk that reflects a bond sector’s contribution to the overall duration of the portfolio.

Corporate Debt: Debt instruments issued by private corporations.

Covered Bonds: Debt securities backed by cash flows from pools of mortgages or public sector loans. The asset pools help secure or “cover” these bonds if the originating financial institution becomes insolvent.

Coupon Interest Rate: The interest rate that is assigned to an interest-paying fixed income security when it is issued.

Credit Analysis: An analysis of the financial strength of the issuer of a security, and the ability of that issuer to provide timely payment of interest and principal.

Credit Default Swap: Credit derivative contracts between two counterparties that can be used to hedge credit risk or speculate on the changes in the credit quality of a corporation or government entity.

Credit Quality: Measures(usually in terms of high or low) the ability of issuers of debt securities to make timely interest and principal payments.

Credit Risk: The risk that the inability of the issuers of debt securities to make payments will cause these securities to decline in value.

Currency Overlay: A financial trading strategy used to separate the management of currency risk from other portfolio strategies. A currency

overlay manager can seek to hedge the risk from adverse movements in exchange rates, and/or attempt to profit from tactical currency views.

Debt Service: The amount of money required within a given period to keep current on required/scheduled repayments of outstanding debt, including interest and principal.

Deflation: A decline in prices for goods, assets and services.

Derivatives: Securities whose performance and/or structure is derived from the performance and/or structure of other assets, interest rates or indexes.

Duration: A measure of the price sensitivity of a fixed income investment to changes in interest rates. The longer the duration, expressed in years, the more a fixed income investment’s price will change when interest rates change.

Emerging Markets (EM) Debt: Debt issued by countries whose economies are considered to be developing or emerging from underdevelopment.

Excess Return: The return of an investment portfolio minus the return of what is considered to be a relatively risk-free asset, such as a U.S. Treasury bill.

Exchange-Traded Fund (ETF): Represents a group of securities but is traded on an exchange like an individual stock.

Federal Funds Rate: An overnight interest rate banks charge each other for loans.

Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC): The committee that sets interest rate and credit policies for the Federal Reserve System, the U.S. central bank.

Federal Reserve (Fed): The U.S. central bank, responsible for monetary policies affecting the U.S. financial system and the economy.

Fundamental Factors: Economic or financial data used in determining asset value.

Futures Contracts (Futures): Agreements to buy or sell a specific amount of a commodity or financial instrument at a particular price on a stipulated future date.

General Obligation (GO) Bonds: Municipal bonds that are secured by the full faith and credit of the issuer, including its taxing power.

Gross Domestic Product (GDP): A measure of the total economic output in goods and services for an economy.

Government-Sponsored Enterprises (GSEs): Privately owned corporations created by Congress to provide funding and help to reduce the cost of capital for certain borrowing sectors of the economy such as homeowners, students and farmers.

Headline Risk: Refers to the risk that a negative news media headline about one security issuer, incident or sector could affect the demand for and pricing of a much wider swath of securities.

Hedge: An investment designed to reduce the risk of an adverse price move in another investment. Often a hedge consists of taking an offsetting position in a related investment, such as a futures contract.

High-Yield (HY) Debt: Fixed income securities with lower credit quality and lower credit ratings. High-yield securities are rated below BBB-.

Hybrid Adjustable-Rate Mortgages (ARMs): Combine the characteristics of fixed-rate and adjustable-rate mortgages, with rates remaining fixed for a set period of time and then adjusting periodically according to a specific index.

Inflation: An economic condition of rising prices for goods, services and assets, or equivalently, a declining value of money. Core inflation excludes food and energy prices, which tend to be volatile. It is the opposite of Deflation.

Inflation-Indexed Securities: Debt securities that offer returns adjusted for inflation. Typically, the principal of inflation-indexed securities is indexed to a widely used inflation measure and adjusted accordingly. Interest payments can be adjusted as well.

Interest Rate Risk: The risk that a fixed income investment’s value will change due to changes in interest rates.

Investment-Grade (IG) Debt: Fixed income securities with relatively high credit quality and credit ratings in the upper ranges of those provided by credit rating services. Using Standard & Poor’s ratings as the benchmark, investment-grade securities are those rated BBB- or higher.

Leverage: The use of financial instruments and/or borrowed capital to increase potential returns or to increase purchasing power.

Long Position: Typical ownership of an asset or investment that gives the owner the right to transfer ownership, the right to any income generated by the asset, and the right to any profits or losses due to value changes.

Long/Short Position: A “market neutral” investing strategy that involves taking long positions in assets that are expected to increase in value and short positions in assets that are expected to decrease in value.

Master Limited Partnerships (MLPs): Publicly traded, generally higher- yielding securities of enterprises that engage in certain businesses, usually pertaining to the use of natural resources.

Mortgage-Backed Securities (MBS): A form of securitized debt that represents ownership in pools of mortgage loans and their payments.

MSCI ACWI (All Country World Index): A free float-adjusted market capitalization-weighted index that is designed to measure the equity market performance of developed and emerging markets.

Municipal Securities: Debt securities typically issued by or on behalf of U.S. state and local governments, their agencies or authorities to raise money for a variety of public purposes, including financing for state and local governments as well as financing for specific projects and public facilities.

Nominal Yield: The interest rate to par value that the bond issuer promises to pay bond purchasers.

Non-Agency Mortgage-Backed Securities (MBS): MBS are groups of home mortgages that are sold by the issuing banks and then packaged together into "pools" and sold as a single security. Non-agency MBS are issued by private entities, such as financial institutions.

Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE): This price deflator which comes from the Bureau of Economic Analysis' quarterly report on U.S. gross domestic product is based on a survey of businesses and is intended to capture the price changes in all final goods, no matter the purchaser. Because of its broader scope and certain differences in the methodology used to calculate the PCE price index, the Federal Reserve holds the PCE deflator as its preferred, consistent measure of inflation over time.

Quantitative Easing (QE): A form of monetary policy used by central banks to stimulate economic growth by purchasing domestic government securities to increase the domestic money supply, lower interest rates and encourage investors to make investments in riskier assets such as stocks and high-yield securities.

Real Estate Investment Trusts (REITs): Securities that trade like stocks and invest in real estate through properties or mortgages.

Real Yield: A yield that has been adjusted to remove the effects of inflation.

Revenue Bonds: Municipal bonds that are secured by the net revenues from the project or facility being financed.

S&P 500 Index: The S&P 500® Index is composed of 500 selected common stocks most of which are listed on the New York Stock Exchange. It is not an investment product available for purchase.

Secondary Market: A market where investors make purchases from other investors, rather than from the initial issuers.

Securitized Debt: Debt resulting from aggregating debt instruments into a pool of similar debts, then issuing new securities backed by the pool.

ABS, MBS, and CMOs are common forms of securitized debt.

Short Position: Refers to the sale of an asset borrowed, not owned, by the seller in anticipation of a price decline. If the seller can buy the asset later(to return what was borrowed) at a lower price, a profit results. If the price rises, the borrower/seller suffers a loss.

Sovereign Debt: A country's government-issued debt, priced in its native currency, which can be sold to investors in other countries.

Spreads, Maturity Spreads, Credit Spreads: Measured differences between two interest rates or yields that are being compared with each other. Spreads typically are measured between fixed income securities of the same credit quality, but different maturities(maturity spreads), or of the same maturity, but different credit quality(credit spreads).

Spread Sectors: Non-Treasury debt sectors with securities that usually trade at higher yields than comparable-maturity U.S. Treasury securities. These sectors typically trade at higher yields than Treasuries because they usually have relatively higher credit risk and/or more risk of prepayment.

Spread Widening, Tightening: Changes in spreads that reflect changes in relative value, with spread widening usually indicating relative price depreciation and spread tightening indicating relative price appreciation.

Swaps: Two-party contracts entered into primarily by institutional investors for periods ranging from a few weeks to more than one year. In a standard “swap” transaction, two parties agree to exchange the returns(or differentials in rates of return) earned or realized on particular predetermined investments or instruments.

Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities (TIPS): Inflation-linked debt securities issued by the U.S. Treasury.

Technical Factors: Market price behavior, trends, volume, and momentum data used in determining asset value.

U.S. Treasury securities: Debt securities issued by the U.S. Treasury and backed by the direct "full faith and credit" pledge of the U.S. government. Treasury securities include bills (maturing in one year or less), notes (maturing in two to 10 years) and bonds(maturing in more than 10 years). They are generally considered among the highest quality and most liquid securities in the world.

Variable-Rate Demand Notes (VRDNs): Short-term debt securities that track market interest rates using periodic(daily, weekly, monthly, or quarterly) interest rate adjustments.

Weighted Average Life to Maturity: The average time in years to receive the principal repayments.

Yield: A rate of return for bonds and other fixed-income securities. There are several types of yields and yield calculations.

Yield Curve: A line graph showing the yields of fixed income securities from a single sector from a range of different maturities at a single point in time.

Yield to Maturity: A common performance calculation for fixed income securities, which takes into account total annual interest payments, the purchase price, the redemption value, and the amount of time remaining until maturity.

Real Yield to Maturity: Yield to maturity minus any "inflation premium" that had been added/priced in.

Zero-Coupon Securities: Debt securities that are sold at a deep discount then redeemed for their full face value at maturity.

Glossary Sources: American Century Investments, Bloomberg Indices.

data.title: Oklo: Too Far, Too Fast (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
A number of months ago, we wrote a bullish article on Oklo (NYSE:OKLO), the early-stage nuclear reactor company.

Titled "Oklo: We're Betting Heavily On This 'Boom Or Bust' Nuclear Reactor Startup", we came out as strong bulls in the stock, on the back of the company's robust deal pipeline, promising regulatory situation, and - at the time - reasonable looking valuation.

Plus, from a financial point of view, the company's potential unit economics appeared enticing, with strong free cash flow generation looking achievable by the end of the decade.

While OKLO hasn't yet delivered a working 'Powerhouse', we thought the company, which was trading at projected 17.6x 2030 cash flow, looked like it was worth a shot.

And 'worth a shot' it has been.

Since our article, shares are up more than 160% from early December, as speculative hype around AI power consumption and demand has materialized:

Obviously, right now, the company has no meaningful revenues.

This means that all of the growth in the stock price is due to multiple expansion, which reflects investor speculation around the company's prospects.

Clearly, this isn't a formula for long-term alpha, so in this article, we wanted to revisit the stock to see whether or not it is still worth your time and money.

Today, we'll explore OKLO's outlook, dive into the valuation, and explain why we think that shares are no longer attractive for new buyers but should be 'held' by investors who have bought into the long-term vision.

Sound good? Let's dive in.

OKLO's Thesis & Financials

Before we look at the valuation, it's worth reminding ourselves what OKLO does and why the market has given the company - which has yet to produce a working product - so much financial 'rope'.

As we see it, the core thesis for OKLO is built around society's long-term demand for green, sustainable power. Until battery technology gets better, many of the world's renewable power sources, like solar and wind, produce intermittently, which doesn't suit today's grid demand curve.

Similarly, non-intermittent sources, like Hydro and Geothermal, don't have the capacity to fuel, nominally, global power demand. Thus, we're stuck with a continued mix of nuclear (which is green, but expensive), and coal and natural gas (which are inexpensive, but not green).

As we see it, OKLO is a company that has the potential to stem this gap, providing cheap, clean nuclear power on a localized level, leading to a more robust, sustainable grid that can keep up with growing demand. With AI and EVs - along with population growth - serving as core demand drivers in the coming decade, it's clear that humanity needs to find a solution, and fast.

Enter the 'Powerhouse':

This is OKLO's core product, which is still in the regulatory / design stage.

The company thinks that its smaller, less capital-intensive nuclear reactors have better unit economics than huge plants, and they might be right. Big plants have complicated design schemes and huge upfront Capex. In combination with extensive permitting and regulatory processes which can take decades to wade through, setting up a new nuclear power plant in today's day and age can be quite unattractive.

But what if you could mass produce a smaller, pre-approved reactor design and slim the permitting process down to just a few years?

This is what OKLO is looking to do, and the company has already had some success.

While skeptics may argue that bulls are underestimating the regulatory hurdles, the fact remains that OKLO has pioneered a new regulatory pathway for its 'Powerhouses', which could drive faster rollout and deployment for the company over the next 10 years:

Not only that, but OKLO plans on designing, producing, and operating Powerhouses in a single integrated chain, verticalizing the power production process and maximizing efficiency. This should allow the company to compete on power pricing, as well as operating costs.

Finally, the unit economics of Powerhouses themselves appear attractive, with management forecasting sequential 15 and 50 MW powerhouses producing somewhere in the range of high teens to low 20s cash on cash unlevered returns:

With a projected payback period in the 4-8 year range, depending on the size and upfront cost, if Powerhouses can last for even 15 years (which would be incredibly pessimistic), then you're looking at a cash-cow business that's singularly integrated.

Even if you assume that everything will take twice as long and be twice as expensive, then you're still looking at relatively attractive ongoing financial results, scaling over time.

Thus, our ongoing 'bullish' thesis for OKLO's underlying business.

OKLO's Valuation

So, what's changed?

Why are we downgrading the stock to a 'Hold'?

In short, the risk/reward of investing into OKLO has changed materially in the last few months.

In other words, the risks have remained the same, but the potential rewards have fallen.

Since our first article, OKLO's stock has rallied more than 150% on the back of a number of positive developments.

First off, in mid-December, OKLO signed a deal with Switch, a huge data center operator:

Nuclear technology company Oklo on Wednesday reported a non-binding master power agreement with Switch, to deploy 12 gigawatts of Oklo Aurora powerhouse projects through 2044, sending shares up in early trade.

Then came a partnership with RPower, a Texas-based prime and backup solutions company, which focused on providing a nat-gas / nuclear 'phased energy model' for data centers:

The phased power model is designed to work in three stages: At first, RPower’s natural gas generators would be deployed within 24 months, depending on site conditions, to meet immediate power needs for data centers that the local utility cannot serve, then Oklo's Aurora nuclear power solutions would be added to the sites as they become commercially available, providing emissions-free energy, then over time the Aurora powerhouses would supply the majority of the energy needed.

Finally, OKLO announced another partnership with Lightbridge (LTBR), this time focused on nuclear fuel recycling:

Lightbridge and Oklo [traded] to an all-time intraday high ... in Thursday's trading after disclosing the companies signed a memorandum of understanding to jointly explore opportunities for reprocessing and recycling spent uranium-zirconium fuel.

This all comes amid a backdrop of highly bullish analyst sentiment for energy demand on the whole.

To some, these deals and partnerships may look like they de-risk the company from a business perspective - which could thus drive a higher multiple - but all they really do is bring in positive news flow to the company.

Sure, we give props to OKLO's business development team for driving these deals, but at the end of the day, none of it matters if the firm can't design, permit, deploy, and operate a Powerhouse within the next few years.

Thus, in our view, as these deals haven't made this process any easier, we see the risk story as being largely the same as before.

When you tack on the fact that the company's financials haven't moved one iota, the valuation starts to become a concern.

In our first article, we wrote the following:

We'd say that a base case for OKLO would be ~10 powerhouses by the end of 2030. At that point, the company could be producing as much as $134 million in cash flow, with little in the way of other operational overhead.

Given the $2.4 billion valuation, this means that OKLO is trading, tentatively, at 17.6x 2030 cash flow.

Now, at the time of writing, OKLO is trading at a valuation of $6.42 billion. If you assume that our $134 million in cash flow projection remains the 2030 'base case', then suddenly the company's valuation has moved from 17.6x to 47x 2030 cash flow.

What a jump.

For a company that is currently bringing in basically zero revenue, the story has gone from 'boom or bust' to 'priced to perfection'. For this valuation to make sense, OKLO needs to perform, in our view, flawlessly when it comes to execution.

Given that the company is working on bringing a never-before-seen nuclear reactor product to market, we think the chance of flawless execution, from a relatively inexperienced management team, is very low.

Summary

We're not saying that OKLO will be a long-term failure. In fact, we think that the stock will likely be a long-term winner. However, as we look at it, purely from a pricing point of view, we don't think it makes sense to deploy additional capital into the company at this time.

The risk/reward has shifted, and much of the short-run upside has already been captured.

Thus, our downgrade to a 'Hold' rating.

Stay safe out there!
